trajectory.description: Observation: empty workspace. Analysis: need a fast end-to-end baseline that satisfies eval contract and gives an initial score/time measurement. Decision: implement a custom CNN encoder with width-preserving downsampling plus attention LSTM decoder, train for 3 epochs, save checkpoint, and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: first 3-epoch baseline completed but took ~2.7h and hidden exprate was 0; logged val loss around 2.8, so the model is still far from sequence-exact recognition and the run was too slow for extensive exploration. Analysis: the main bottlenecks are high-resolution images and greedy validation over the full val set; within the remaining ~3.3h the best chance is to continue training from the existing checkpoint, remove expensive exact-match validation during training, use AMP/gradient clipping, and save by teacher-forced validation loss. Decision: patch train.py for resume+AMP+fast val-loss-only mode and continue from the current checkpoint for additional epochs at slightly lower resolution to fit the remaining time, then submit the updated checkpoint.

trajectory.command: python build_retrieval.py --out checkpoints/retrieval_db.pt && python predict.py --data_path /data/val --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output /tmp/preds.json

Check the documentation of torch.load to learn more about types accepted by default with weights_only https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.load.html.
trajectory.description: Observation: after two long neural training attempts the hidden exprate remains 0 and only ~1h remains. Analysis: continuing the slow seq2seq model is unlikely to reach exact-expression accuracy in time; a retrieval fallback using compact visual features from labeled train/val data can produce valid LaTeX and may recover exact matches/near-duplicates or simple expressions. Decision: build a compact nearest-neighbor label database from train+val images, patch predict.py to use retrieval by default with optional neural fallback disabled, then submit immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: retrieval database was built successfully, but predict.py failed because torch.load now defaults to weights_only=True and rejects the numpy arrays stored in retrieval_db.pt. Analysis: this is a loading compatibility issue, not a modeling issue; setting weights_only=False for this trusted workspace artifact should fix prediction and allow evaluation. Decision: patch predict.py to load the retrieval DB with weights_only=False, run a local prediction smoke test on val images, then submit evaluation.

Observation: retrieval now runs but hidden exprate is still 0, with only small edit-distance hits. Analysis: exact retrieval is unlikely to solve CROHME test; the previous attention decoder trained too slowly and did not learn enough. A simpler left-to-right CNN+BiGRU+CTC recognizer can train much faster and may exactly solve some simple expressions, while keeping retrieval as fallback if needed. Decision: add a CTC training script and patch predict.py to recognize CTC checkpoints; train a compact CTC model on train+val for a time-bounded run, then submit the CTC checkpoint for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: CTC training completed within the remaining time and saved checkpoints/ctc.pt, but no evaluation has been submitted yet. The visible predict.py excerpt suggests it may still default to retrieval when a retrieval_db.pt exists in the checkpoint directory, which would prevent evaluating the new CTC checkpoint. Analysis: with only ~8.6 minutes left, I need to quickly inspect predict.py/ctc_train.py to confirm the checkpoint format and prediction dispatch before submitting. Decision: inspect the relevant code sections with minimal command time, then patch/submit in the next iteration.

trajectory.description: Observation: predict.py still checks retrieval before loading the submitted checkpoint, so submitting checkpoints/ctc.pt would evaluate the old retrieval baseline rather than the newly trained CTC model. Analysis: the CTC checkpoint contains type='ctc' and requires a matching CTCModel definition/decoder inside predict.py. Decision: replace predict.py with a dispatching version that first loads the checkpoint, runs CTC decoding when type=='ctc', and only uses retrieval for non-CTC checkpoints; then submit checkpoints/ctc.pt immediately due to the very small remaining time budget.

trajectory.command:
python predict.py --data_path /data/val --checkpoint checkpoints/ctc.pt --output val_ctc_pred.json && python - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path
labs={}
for line in open('/data/val/labels.txt',encoding='utf-8'):
    if line.strip():
        k,v=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t',1); labs[k]=v
pred=json.load(open('val_ctc_pred.json'))['predictions']
ex=sum(1 for p in pred if labs.get(p['id'])==p['latex'])
empty=sum(1 for p in pred if not p['latex'].strip())
print({'n':len(pred),'exact':ex,'empty':empty,'sample':pred[:5]})
PY
trajectory.description: Observation: CTC submission improved near-miss rate (exprate_le2 0.0125) but exact exprate remains 0, and only about 8 minutes remain. Analysis: the most plausible quick gain is prediction-side calibration/postprocessing rather than more training. I need local validation diagnostics to see whether CTC, retrieval, or simple post-correction has any exact matches on the visible val set before risking a final patch. Decision: run a compact local validation comparison using the existing checkpoint/predict path and labels, with minimal stdout.

Observation: the CTC checkpoint currently predicts empty strings for 982/986 visible validation images, so the issue is likely blank dominance during greedy CTC decoding rather than an eval-contract problem. Analysis: with under 8 minutes left, the best remaining option is to calibrate decoding by subtracting a blank logit penalty and compare local validation exact/empty rates. Decision: run a quick grid search over blank penalties on the validation split using the existing checkpoint to pick a safer predict-time setting or fallback strategy.

trajectory.description: Observation: blank-penalty calibration did not yield any visible validation exact matches; the CTC model is effectively unusable for exact decoding, while pure retrieval also had hidden exact 0 but at least returns plausible full expressions. Analysis: with only ~7 minutes left, the best remaining chance is a prediction-side ensemble/fallback: use CTC only when it produces a nontrivial expression, otherwise fall back to visual retrieval. This cannot hurt empty CTC predictions for exact match unless the true expression is empty, which is implausible, and may recover cases where retrieval is exact. Decision: patch predict.py to dispatch CTC with retrieval fallback for empty/brace-only outputs and submit immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: all submitted neural/retrieval variants still have hidden exprate 0, while remaining time is only about 2-3 minutes. Analysis: no further training is feasible; the only plausible remaining action is a very small prediction-side prior for extremely simple narrow expressions, because a constant/common single-symbol guess can potentially recover at least one exact expression whereas current retrieval has zero exact. I need a quick look at the available label distribution before choosing the fallback token. Decision: compute the most common short labels from the saved retrieval database/training labels with a minimal command, then patch predict.py and submit in the next iteration if time permits.

trajectory.description: Observation: hidden exact remains 0 for all trained models/retrieval, and only ~2 minutes remain. Analysis: further training is impossible; the only feasible chance is a prediction-side heuristic for very small/single-symbol expressions, where nearest-neighbor over all full expressions may be distracted. Decision: overwrite predict.py with a robust retrieval predictor that overrides small cropped images using nearest neighbors restricted to short labels, then submit immediately.
